intro:
A garden in Scripture is a cultivated, watered, enclosed place of life and beauty — but more than horticulture is at stake. The garden is where God and humanity dwell together. The Bible opens with one and closes with its restoration, so that the garden becomes a picture of the whole hope of communion with God: a sheltered space, full of life, where his presence is enjoyed without shame.

It begins in Eden. The LORD God plants a garden and places the man there "to work it and keep it," with every tree pleasant to the sight and good for food, and the tree of life at its center (Genesis 2:8–9). A river flows out to water the earth, and God walks there in the cool of the day — Eden is the first sanctuary, the meeting place of heaven and earth. But sin shatters that communion: the man and woman are driven out, and cherubim with a flaming sword guard the way back to the tree of life (Genesis 3:23–24). The whole story that follows is, in one sense, the long road back into the garden.

The prophets keep the image alive as the shape of redemption. The LORD promises to comfort Zion and make "her wilderness like Eden, her desert like the garden of the LORD" (Isaiah 51:3), and Ezekiel foresees the desolate land becoming "like the garden of Eden" (Ezekiel 36:35). The temple, planted with palm and flower carvings and guarded by cherubim, was itself a garden in stone, signaling the return of Eden's presence.

The shadow moves toward its substance in Christ, who reverses Eden in a garden of his own. Where the first Adam fell, the last Adam submits to the Father in a garden across the Kidron (John 18:1); he is crucified and laid in a tomb set in a garden (John 19:41), and on the first day of the week the risen Lord is mistaken for "the gardener" (John 20:15) — the new Man in the new garden of resurrection life. Yet the full flowering waits for the new creation, where the consummation is itself a garden-city: the river of the water of life flows from God's throne and the tree of life bears its fruit, its leaves "for the healing of the nations" (Revelation 22:1–2).

So the garden frames the whole Bible — paradise lost in Eden, paradise regained in the new Jerusalem — and assures the believer that the communion broken by sin will be restored, fuller than before, in a renewed creation where God again dwells with his people.
